result.description: Adversarial statistics + policy-coherence review of the D1 quality-floor / PF1 / stripped-estimator batch

findings.title: Quality-floor miss collapses into a TERMINAL, write-once FAIL indistinguishable from "no skill" (wrong verdict semantics)
findings.detail: loop.py:604-613 folds the D1 product floor into the SAME OOSOutcome.FAIL returned when the significance test fails (a.validates False) or excess-Sharpe<=0. But those encode fundamentally different facts: significance-FAIL = "no edge shown"; floor-miss = "a real, significant, buy-and-hold-beating edge exists, just below the owner's magnitude bar" (the diff comment itself says '"skill exists, but below what you call validated"'). The lockbox treats both identically as terminal and write-once: service.py:132-133/172-179 write an OOSResultRow, consume one unit of the lineage's scarce OOS budget, and can never be overwritten (AlreadyEvaluatedError). So a validated-but-modest strategy is (1) permanently recorded as a plain FAIL with no way for a reader to tell it from a rejected-for-no-skill candidate, and (2) unrecoverable — the lineage's OOS allowance is burned. This contradicts the batch's own honesty doctrine: Track-1 pushed underpowered->UNEVALUATED and FB3 records an honesty-note rather than overloading FAIL. A floor miss is a real measurement (so UNEVALUATED is also wrong), but it needs a DISTINCT recorded outcome (e.g. BELOW_FLOOR / VALIDATED_SUBTHRESHOLD) carried in extras AND surfaced, and it should not be terminal in the same write-once, budget-consuming sense as a genuine significance failure. Recommendation: return a distinct outcome for the floor arm (keep PASS/FAIL for the significance+excess arms), or at minimum stamp extras with a floor_miss=True sub-reason and make the lockbox record it non-terminally / retryably.

findings.title: Floor keys on the raw OOS point estimate, not a CI bound → terminally fails a true-1.0 edge ~44% of the time
findings.detail: loop.py:607 gates on `float(m.get('sharpe_annual',0.0)) >= floor` — the OOS window's observed annualized Sharpe, a point estimate whose SE ~= sqrt(252/T) is ~1.0 at a 1y hold-out. Simulated (iid gaussian, venv python): P(observed < 0.9 | true = 1.0) = 0.466 / 0.463 / 0.446 / 0.425 at OOS = 0.5 / 1 / 2 / 3 years; even a true 1.2 edge fails 41% / 38% / 33% / 30%; a true-1.0 edge measures below 0.7 in 42% / 38% / 34% / 30% of runs. The miss rate is nearly length-invariant because the floor gap (0.1) is dwarfed by the estimate noise. Combined with the terminal/write-once semantics above, ~4 in 10 genuinely-1.0 strategies are permanently killed by OOS estimation noise. The confidence assessment already computes ci_low/ci_high for the observed Sharpe (loop.py:572-579, plumbed into OOSResult at 633-634) — the floor should consume it: fail only when the floor lies ABOVE the CI upper bound ("we can statistically rule out that this reaches your bar"), which converts an uncontrolled point comparison into a defensible test and drops the false-negative rate at the boundary from ~50% toward the CI level. A floor-with-retry (re-evaluate on a longer window before terminal write) is the alternative. Secondary: m['sharpe_annual'] on the hold-out path is the geometric/compounded Sharpe (see loop.py:520-526), which runs ~0.08 below the arithmetic Sharpe the DSR/significance machinery reasons in for sigma=1%/day — so the nominal 0.9 floor is effectively ~0.98 on the arithmetic scale, stricter than the owner's knob reads and scale-inconsistent with the rest of the stack.

findings.title: PF5 freeze artifact references a power-curve sha256 that does not match the file on disk, and nothing enforces it
findings.detail: coverage-v2-gate-config.json d1_resolution.evidence_power_curve_sha256_prefix = "f2cb94079b4658a7", but sha256(docs/design/coverage-v2-power-curve.json) on disk = "2e5ff32b13bf8ec7". The claimed prefix equals the hash of the in-memory json.dumps(...) blob the script prints (preflight_power_curve.py:110-115), NOT the bytes actually written: Path.write_text on Windows CRLF-translates '\n', so the file bytes differ from the hashed blob (verified: blob.encode()==raw is False). Consequences: (1) anyone verifying the "write-once, tamper-evident" evidence chain with `sha256sum coverage-v2-power-curve.json` gets a mismatch against the frozen provenance; (2) the tamper trip-wire test (test_gate_config_freeze.py) only asserts the DSR/OOS/coverage CONSTANTS and never checks this evidence hash, so the provenance link in the freeze artifact is decorative and already inconsistent. Fix: hash the file bytes (write bytes / newline='' , or hash after write) and add a freeze-test assertion that re-hashes the on-disk power curve against the recorded prefix, or drop the prefix rather than record an unverifiable one.

findings.title: Policy incoherence: d1_resolution calls the OOS lockbox "the reliable confirmer of the 0.9-1.0 band" while PF1 + the floor's own miss rate show that band is unconfirmable
findings.detail: coverage-v2-gate-config.json d1_resolution.meaning states the OOS lockbox is "the reliable confirmer of the 0.9-1.0 band." PF1's own curve (coverage-v2-power-curve.json) shows the in-sample DSR gate has ZERO power at edges 0.9 and 1.0 at every operating point using the full T=2265-bar window; the OOS hold-out is a strict subset (loop.py:708-709, oos_start=window_end) so it has strictly less data. My floor simulation confirms the OOS confirmer is a coin flip on true-1.0 edges (~44% terminal FAIL). So the artifact's central justification — moving 0.9-1.0 confirmation from the (provably powerless) in-sample gate to the OOS lockbox — asks the OOS stage to do reliably what more data provably cannot, and the implementation does not achieve it. The claim should be softened to what is true ("the OOS floor RECORDS whether the observed hold-out Sharpe clears the product bar; it is a noisy point comparison, not a powered confirmer"), and the FB4 top-K rationing it defers to should own the actual 0.9-band selection.

findings.title: PF1 harness passes the planted (noise-free) edge as metrics['sharpe_annual'] — inert today, a latent trap
findings.detail: preflight_power_curve.py:63 sets metrics={'sharpe_annual': edge, ...}. Verified this is NOT a correctness issue for the curve: DeflatedSharpeGate computes sr_hat from ctx.returns (deflated_sharpe.py:44, r.mean()/r.std) and reads ctx.metrics only for exposure_time (deflated_sharpe.py:124); metrics['sharpe_annual'] is never consumed, so the realized sample Sharpe (which the gate needs) is correctly derived from the returns that carry mu=edge/sqrt(252)*sigma. But planting the true edge as the metric is misleading dead data: if any future gate consumer reads metrics['sharpe_annual'], the harness would silently feed it the noise-free planted value instead of the realized one, invalidating the curve without any test noticing. Recommend dropping the field or setting it to the realized returns' Sharpe.

findings.title: market_mode_stripped_effective_count: 1+share*PR form is defensible; the original-t_obs-on-rank-reduced-residual caveat does not bite empirically
findings.detail: The variance-share weighting (effective_n.py: `1.0 + resid_share * pr_resid`) is theoretically heuristic and passes the ORIGINAL t_obs into mp_denoised_effective_count on a rank-reduced, renormalized residual (whose MP null q=n/T no longer matches after one factor is removed), so in principle surviving noise eigenvalues could inflate pr_resid when resid_share~=1. Stress-tested with the venv (n=30): pure idiosyncratic noise -> 28.85 (vs raw 29.80), weak-beta0.2 -> 27.96, T=200 -> 23.87 — the count never inflates above n and the MP denoiser holds, so no pathology is realized. The near-zero-residual-diagonal path (clip to 1e-12 then divide) is guarded and only reached when a series is fully explained by the market mode. Combined with the documented "over-count is the safe direction" (it can only reduce the raw visited N less) and the estimator being UNWIRED, this is acceptable as built. No change required for this batch; if it is ever wired, re-derive the MP threshold on the residual's reduced rank (t_obs for an (n-1)-rank matrix) before trusting pr_resid near share~1.

result.summary: Reviewed the D1 quality-floor / PF1 power-curve / stripped-estimator batch on a statistics + policy-coherence lens, verifying numerically with the venv. Two must-fix issues around the quality floor: (BLOCKER) a floor miss returns the SAME terminal, write-once, budget-consuming OOSOutcome.FAIL as a genuine no-skill rejection, conflating \"validated but modest\" with \"no edge\" and making it unrecoverable — it needs a distinct, non-terminal recorded outcome (matching the Track-1/FB3 honesty doctrine); (HIGH) the floor gates on the raw OOS point estimate, which — simulated — terminally fails a true-annualized-1.0 edge ~44% of the time (42-47% across 0.5-4y hold-outs) because the 0.1 floor gap is dwarfed by the ~1.0 estimate SE, so it should key on the already-computed CI upper bound (or retry) instead. A third HIGH: the PF5 freeze artifact records a power-curve sha256 (f2cb94079b4658a7) that does not match the file on disk (2e5ff32b, Windows CRLF-translated by write_text), and the tamper test never checks it, breaking the provenance chain. Plus a MEDIUM policy-coherence gap (the artifact calls the OOS lockbox a \"reliable confirmer\" of a band PF1 shows is unconfirmable), and two LOWs: the PF1 harness feeds the planted edge as metrics['sharpe_annual'] (verified inert for the DSR gate, which uses returns only — a latent trap, not a curve bug), and the stripped estimator's 1+share*PR form is defensible with no pathological inflation found in stress tests (safe-direction over-count, still unwired).

findings.title: Effective OOS-floor default lives in three unsynced literals; PF5 freeze test pins only one → drift the tamper-wire is meant to prevent
findings.detail: loop.OOS_MIN_SHARPE_DEFAULT (loop.py:279) is the ONLY source pinned by test_gate_config_freeze.py (test_oos_lockbox_constants_match_the_freeze, line 30). But router.py:86 (StartRunRequest.oos_min_sharpe = 0.9) and run.py:155 (oos_min_sharpe: float = 0.9) hardcode the value as bare literals, and both ALWAYS pass their value down the chain (router.py:454, run.py:347). So in production the loop-level OOS_MIN_SHARPE_DEFAULT is never the effective default — the two literals are. A deliberate re-freeze that lowers/raises OOS_MIN_SHARPE_DEFAULT together with coverage-v2-gate-config.json will PASS the freeze test while the real API and CLI defaults silently stay 0.9. That is exactly the silent-retune the never-loosen invariant + tamper test claim to forbid, and it is not noted in the freeze test (task item e confirmed: NOT covered). Fix: import OOS_MIN_SHARPE_DEFAULT in router.py/run.py as the default (single source), or extend the freeze test to assert StartRunRequest.model_fields['oos_min_sharpe'].default == run_research default == loop.OOS_MIN_SHARPE_DEFAULT == config value. Not a runtime bug today (all three are 0.9), hence medium, but it defeats a stated safety property.

findings.title: StartRunRequest.oos_min_sharpe has no Pydantic Field(ge/le); API silently accepts out-of-band values and clamps them
findings.detail: router.py:86 declares `oos_min_sharpe: float = 0.9` with no bounds. The clamp is only applied later inside _oos_verdict (loop.py:606). Correctness is preserved (the [0.5,1.0] clamp protects the never-loosen invariant, verified by test_quality_floor_is_configurable_within_the_band_and_clamped), so this is not a semantics bug. But a caller POSTing oos_min_sharpe=99.0 or -5 gets a silent 200 and an effective floor of 1.0 / 0.5 rather than a 422 — surprising and undocumented. Task item f: acceptable as-is for safety, but adding Field(ge=0.5, le=1.0) gives a clear validation error and makes the band self-documenting at the API boundary; the internal clamp can stay as defence-in-depth.

findings.title: D1 quality-floor knob is API-only; not wired in the frontend despite being described as a 'frontend knob'
findings.detail: coverage-v2-gate-config.json ('frontend knob') and the loop.py:275 comment describe oos_min_sharpe as a per-run frontend slider. grep shows no frontend reference to oos_min_sharpe, while the sibling run knobs coverage_dsr/coverage_memory ARE wired in frontend/src/app/dashboard/research/new/page.tsx. Consequence: every real run currently uses the 0.9 API default; the 0.5-1.0 configurability the owner chose is reachable only via direct API/CLI callers. Out of scope of this backend diff, but flagged as a delivery gap so the UI slider isn't assumed present.

findings.title: Power-curve provenance sha is fragile on Windows: declared prefix matches only the LF-canonical form, not the CRLF bytes the script writes
findings.detail: The committed docs/design/coverage-v2-power-curve.json is untracked and was written by the script via Path.write_text, which on Windows emits CRLF; its raw-byte sha256 is 2e5ff32b13bf8ec7. The config's declared evidence_power_curve_sha256_prefix and the script's own printed sha (preflight_power_curve.py:114) are computed over the in-memory LF blob = f2cb94079b4658a7. I confirmed re-serializing the file with json.dumps(...,indent=1,sort_keys=True) reproduces f2cb94079b4658a7 (and a full re-run of the script reproduces identical numbers + that sha), so the artifact IS re-derivable. But a reviewer who hashes the committed file bytes directly on Windows will NOT match the declared prefix. Consider writing with newline='\n' (or hashing the file as written) so the provenance sha is verifiable against the on-disk bytes cross-platform.

findings.title: _oos_verdict return annotation still tuple[Any, Any] but function returns a 3-tuple (extras added)
findings.detail: loop.py:551 annotates `-> tuple[Any, Any]` while the function returns (OOSOutcome, assessment, extras) — a 3-tuple (docstring at line 554 correctly says 3 elements). Pre-existing, but the signature line was edited by this diff (min_sharpe param added), so it is a cheap fix to make while here. Cosmetic only; callers unpack three values and pass.

result.summary: Contract/threading is sound. min_sharpe is threaded correctly through router -> run_research -> research_loop -> _run_oos_lockbox -> _oos_verdict; the [0.5,1.0] clamp exists in exactly one place (_oos_verdict, loop.py:606) with no double-clamp, and the H16 recover path correctly replays the persisted terminal verdict without re-running the verdict. No pre-existing _oos_verdict test regresses: every PASS-expecting test uses sharpe_annual 1.4-1.5 (above the 0.9 floor), the floor is applied only AFTER the basis==per_trade gate so UNEVALUATED/thin tests are untouched, and executor.py:195 always emits sharpe_annual so there is no realistic missing-key production path where the verdict flips. Freeze-test parents[4] resolves to the repo root correctly. The power-curve artifact is fully re-derivable: I re-ran preflight_power_curve.py to a temp path and got byte-identical numbers and the identical canonical sha256 f2cb94079b4658a7, which matches the config's declared evidence prefix; committed EDGES match the script. The new market_mode_stripped_effective_count is genuinely unwired. 41 targeted tests pass. No blocker/high findings. The main issue is a MEDIUM single-source-of-truth gap: the effective OOS-floor default is duplicated as bare 0.9 literals in router.py and run.py that always override the loop constant, yet the PF5 tamper test pins only the loop constant — so a re-freeze can pass the freeze test while the real API/CLI default silently stays 0.9 (task item e: not noted in the freeze test). Remaining findings are low (no Pydantic Field bounds on oos_min_sharpe -> silent clamp; the knob is not wired in the frontend despite being called a 'frontend knob'; Windows CRLF makes the raw-file sha differ from the declared LF-canonical prefix; stale tuple[Any,Any] return annotation).
